reasoning: Both decrypt() and decryptKeyInfo() are directly exported from lib/xmlenc.js (lines 333-338) and re-exported verbatim through lib/index.js. No non-default options are required: the RetrievalMethod branch at lines 243-246 is taken automatically whenever no inline EncryptedKey is present under KeyInfo — a standard SAML/XMLEnc document layout. The attacker-controlled value is keyRetrievalMethodUri, read from the RetrievalMethod/@URI XML attribute via getAttribute('URI') at line 245, then concatenated without any escaping into XPath string literals at line 246 and line 272-273. No schema validation, allowlist check, or XPath-safe encoding is applied anywhere before the sink. The only option checked before entry is options.key (the caller's own private key), which is always present for any legitimate caller. The attack requires no special configuration: sending attacker-crafted XML to decrypt() or directly to decryptKeyInfo() is sufficient. A URI value containing a single-quote (e.g. '#x' or '1'='1') breaks the XPath string literal and allows injection of arbitrary XPath subexpressions, enabling the attacker to redirect which EncryptedKey node is selected and to use the server as a decryption oracle against its own RSA private key.
